ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for chile con carne; A0CKW (meat-based stew composite dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for chile con carne as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of chile con carne is approximately 250 g (one bowl), as commonly served in restaurant portions.

nutrient_source.title: Chili con carne, homemade-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173756)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for chili con carne (beef and beans) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent a homemade-style preparation with ground beef, kidney beans, tomatoes, onion, garlic, and chili spices. Adapted to per-100g basis from the USDA reported values. This is the closest approved-source match for a restaurant-style chile con carne as served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Kidney beans (canned) GI = 52; mixed bean and meat stew analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chile con carne was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 26 based on the composite dish methodology: kidney beans (GI ~29, University of Sydney GI Database) dominate the carbohydrate fraction; beef contributes negligible glycemic load; tomato sauce and chili spices have minimal glycemic impact. The composite GI is weighted toward the bean fraction, consistent with published bean-dominant stew GI values (range 20–35) in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (26 × 25.5) / 100 = 6.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~950 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size carefully, as a standard 250 g restaurant serving of chile con carne can contribute approximately 950 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the recommended daily limit of 2,400 mg — and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~26) driven by high bean content and moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chile con carne has a low glycemic index of approximately 26 and a glycemic load of ~6.6 per 250 g serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or flour tortillas.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef (~1.9 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat content from ground beef and the high sodium level per serving; choosing lean beef, reducing added salt, and increasing the bean-to-meat ratio can improve the cardiovascular profile of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chile con Carne (also spelled Chili con Carne) is a hearty, slow-cooked meat stew originating from the border cuisine of Mexico and the Southwestern United States. The dish is built on a base of ground or diced beef simmered with kidney beans or pinto beans, crushed tomatoes, onion, garlic, and a blend of dried chili peppers, cumin, and chili powder, producing a rich, deeply spiced, and slightly smoky sauce. The texture is thick and robust, with tender meat and soft beans absorbing the complex spice profile. Chile con Carne is served in Singapore at select Mexican and Tex-Mex restaurants, typically as a main course in a bowl or as a filling for burritos and tacos. Nutritionally, it is a protein-rich and fibre-dense dish: per 100 g it provides approximately 120 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and 10.2 g carbohydrate, with 2.5 g dietary fibre. The high bean content gives it a low glycemic index of approximately 26, making it one of the more diabetes-friendly meat stews available. The primary clinical concern is sodium, with a standard 250 g serving contributing close to 950 mg — relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free in its traditional preparation. Iron content is notable at approximately 2.1 mg per 100 g, contributed by both the beef and the legumes.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or ICMR-NIN entry exists for chile con carne. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 173756), which is an approved secondary global source and the closest defensible match for a beef-and-bean chili stew. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using kidney bean GI values and composite dish methodology, as no direct GI measurement for chile con carne was found. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 173756) for chili con carne (beef and beans), adapted to per-100g basis. This is the closest approved-source match available. 2. GI of 26 is an analogous estimate based on kidney bean GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for chile con carne was found. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this food. 6. This dish is Mexican cuisine, not Singaporean; therefore mandatory multilingual fields for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not required by the Veritas multilingual matrix. Chinese names are provided as the dish is served in Singapore and Chinese-language alias coverage supports retrieval. 7. Halal status is marked false as the beef sourcing at the listed Singapore restaurants (Fiesta Mexico / Three Amigos) is not confirmed halal-certified; this should be verified at the restaurant level. 8. The price range of SGD 20.00 is preserved from the field survey data source record for contextual reference only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
